subUnits.title: 8.1 Physical and Chemical Changes
notes: • Changes that affect the form or appearance of a substance
1. Melting of ice (H₂O solid → H₂O liquid)
2. Boiling of water (H₂O liquid → H₂O gas)
3. Dissolving salt in water (can be recovered by evaporation)
1. Burning of wood (wood → ash + CO₂ + H₂O)
• Magnesium ribbon burns with bright white flame
• Calcium chloride + Sodium carbonate → Calcium carbonate (chalk) + Sodium chloride
• White precipitate of calcium carbonate forms
diagramDescriptions: Comparison chart showing physical vs chemical changes with examples
Burning magnesium ribbon demonstration showing bright white flame and white ash
Rusting of iron nails showing color change from silver to brown
Dissolving salt in water vs burning paper comparison
Flow diagram showing reversibility of physical changes vs irreversibility of chemical changes
subUnits.youtubeQuery: Physical and chemical changes chemistry SSS1 site:youtube.com

quiz.q: A physical change is characterized by:
quiz.explanation: Physical changes do not form new substances. The chemical composition remains the same, only the physical form changes.

quiz.q: Which is a chemical change?
quiz.explanation: Burning magnesium forms magnesium oxide (new substance), making it a chemical change. The others are physical changes.

quiz.q: Rusting of iron is a:
quiz.explanation: Rusting forms iron(III) oxide (Fe₂O₃), a new substance with different properties from iron. It's a chemical change.

quiz.q: Which sign indicates a chemical change?
quiz.explanation: Formation of gas bubbles indicates a new substance is being formed, which is a sign of chemical change.

quiz.q: Dissolving salt in water is a physical change because:
opts: Salt can be recovered by evaporation
quiz.explanation: Salt can be recovered unchanged by evaporating the water, proving no new substance was formed.

quiz.q: Burning of paper is a chemical change because:
opts: Ash and gases are new substances
quiz.explanation: Burning paper produces ash, carbon dioxide, and water vapor - all new substances different from paper.

quiz.q: Which change is reversible?
quiz.explanation: Melting ice is reversible (water can be frozen back to ice). The others are irreversible chemical changes.

quiz.q: Preparation of chalk (CaCO₃) from calcium chloride and sodium carbonate is:
quiz.explanation: CaCl₂ + Na₂CO₃ → CaCO₃ + 2NaCl. A white precipitate of calcium carbonate (new substance) forms, indicating chemical change.

quiz.q: A characteristic of chemical changes is:
quiz.explanation: Chemical changes are usually permanent and irreversible, forming new substances with different properties.

quiz.q: Which involves a chemical change?
quiz.explanation: Digestion breaks down food into simpler substances (chemical change). The others are physical changes.

subUnits.title: 8.2 Atoms, Molecules and Dalton's Atomic Theory
notes: • Smallest particle of an element that can take part in a chemical reaction
• Cannot be divided by chemical means
• Made up of subatomic particles: protons, neutrons, electrons
• Symbol: Chemical symbol represents one atom of an element
• Smallest particle of a substance that can exist independently
• Made up of two or more atoms chemically combined
• Made of same type of atoms
• Made of different types of atoms
John Dalton proposed the first scientific atomic theory with these postulates:
1. All matter is made up of tiny, indivisible particles called atoms
2. Atoms of the same element are identical in mass, size, and properties
3. Atoms of different elements are different in mass, size, and properties
4. Atoms cannot be created, divided, or destroyed in chemical reactions
5. Atoms combine in simple whole number ratios to form compounds
6. Chemical reactions involve rearrangement of atoms
1. Atoms are divisible (contain protons, neutrons, electrons)
2. Atoms of same element can have different masses (isotopes)
3. Atoms can be created or destroyed in nuclear reactions
4. Atoms of different elements can have same mass (isobars)
However, Dalton's theory remains valid for ordinary chemical reactions
• Explained conservation of mass in reactions
diagramDescriptions: Diagram showing atom structure: nucleus with protons and neutrons, electrons in shells
Molecular models: O₂, H₂O, CO₂, NH₃ showing atoms bonded together
Dalton's atomic theory illustration showing atoms as indivisible spheres
Comparison of element molecules (O₂, N₂) vs compound molecules (H₂O, CO₂)
Diagram showing chemical reaction as rearrangement of atoms (before and after)

quiz.q: An atom is:
opts: The smallest particle of an element
quiz.explanation: An atom is the smallest particle of an element that can take part in a chemical reaction.

quiz.q: A molecule is:
opts: Two or more atoms chemically combined
quiz.explanation: A molecule is the smallest particle of a substance that can exist independently, made of two or more atoms chemically bonded.

quiz.q: O₂ represents:
quiz.explanation: O₂ is a molecule of oxygen gas containing two oxygen atoms chemically bonded together.

quiz.q: H₂O contains:
quiz.explanation: A water molecule (H₂O) contains 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom chemically bonded.

quiz.q: According to Dalton, atoms of the same element are:
quiz.explanation: Dalton's theory states that atoms of the same element are identical in mass, size, and properties.

quiz.q: Which statement is part of Dalton's atomic theory?
opts: Atoms combine in whole number ratios
Atoms of same element have different masses
quiz.explanation: Dalton proposed that atoms combine in simple whole number ratios to form compounds.

quiz.q: A molecule of an element contains:
quiz.explanation: Molecules of elements (like O₂, N₂, H₂) contain only one type of atom bonded together.

quiz.q: CO₂ is a molecule of:
quiz.explanation: CO₂ (carbon dioxide) is a compound molecule containing carbon and oxygen atoms (different elements).

quiz.q: Modern science has shown that atoms:
quiz.explanation: Atoms are divisible and contain protons, neutrons, and electrons - a modification to Dalton's theory.

quiz.q: In a chemical reaction, atoms:
quiz.explanation: Chemical reactions involve rearrangement of atoms. Atoms are neither created nor destroyed (conservation of mass).
